0.5 acres; private second growth forest

I am not 100% sure if this is the same thing that is happening in Seward Park or not, but I noticed some sickly, dying, and dead sword ferns in this area recently. It's disturbing in that it's a very rural area without a great deal of human impact. There is an abandoned road grade which elk evidently use in the immediate area; perhaps the elk carried the pathogen in on their hooves.

The worst area (pictured) is about 50' across. There are sickly/dead/dying ferns scattered through the area here and there (not just where pictured). There are no large areas hundreds of feet across with no surviving ferns.

"We observed wilting and dying sword ferns at different micro-sites - from those surrounded by snowberry in more exposed sunny areas, to those in deeper shade under conifer canopy among salal."

"It's easy to overlook, but once you start looking it's pretty prevalent on that site."

Noted large areas of Sword Fern on Artillery Hill in Fort Worden State Park which appear browned and stressed.   Closer inspection indicated plants are alive and creating new growth fiddleheads for the season.   Attached images from Bluff Trail.

This is at the IslandWood environmental education center on Bainbridge Island. I volunteer as a docent there and noticed this apparent example of dieoff while leading a walk on 5 May. One photo shows the dieoff area (about 50 feet across) the other shows a sick fern that's not making any new fronds (it's May; all the healthy ferns have a crop of new fronds by now). Coordinates taken from GPS, not the most accurate since site is under a forest canopy. I did average two readings.

I noticed this area today while on my way to document the site I found on the 5th of this month. This is also on IslandWood property. It's near the buildings along a paved path and is a larger area of dieoff than the other one. Some ferns seem to be surviving, but a lot have died. Coordinates from GPS, and they should be pretty accurate since there was an opening to the sky overhead. E-mail info@islandwood.org to get permission to visit and mention that I am the one who reported this.

Area identified by Al Smith in 2016. Stressed, dead, and healthy sword seems present. Area is roughly 1000 soft. Easy to find from Hazelnut Trail at large cedar stump. Visited previously, and not obviously growing.

column17: Cougar Mountain Regional Wildland Park, sword fern, Galium aparine, Polystichum munitum
column18:
USE CAUTION!  There are several mining pits within this die-off area.  Dangerous fumes are noted to emit from pits in other parts of the park, and they may be a sinkhole risk.   

To the east and within sight of the intersection of Red Town Trail and Rainbow Town Trail, there is what appears to be sword fern - Polystichum munitum - die-off area, perhaps one hundred meters at its widest.  I suspect that it is relatively recent, due to the uncharacteristically thin underbrush that drew me to it when I saw it off-trail.  I would estimate that 75% of the ferns are dead in the area, though there are sections where the die-off is complete.  

This area of forest is relatively young, having been decimated by a history of mining.  It appears to have been colonize by birch, which have completely given over primarily to maple.  There are several small pits in the area that are the legacy of mining, so use caution!  At first I suspected that the dead plants could be a result of mining-related toxins, but the sword ferns were consistently thriving along the rest of the several miles of trails that I explored.  I noticed one odd-looking cleavers plant - Galium aparine - growing out of the base of one dead fern; it had taken on a deep red hue that I can't recall ever having seen before.    

To capture the scope of the die-off, I took two videos which I have uploaded to youtube.  I have no idea why I filmed it in portrait; I beg your forgiveness.  

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Im53Ga4Dbt8&frags=pl%2Cwn

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=l-iuw-LU68Y&frags=pl%2Cwn
Possible patch of sword fern die off on Hartstene Island, near Fudge Point.

column18:
In an approx. 80 year-old, and otherwise very healthy forest that is part of the Dabob Bay Natural Area Preserve, I encountered a portion of the forest understory where patches of Sword Fern appear to have died, and others around them were showing signs of leaf stress and death.  The affected area was just a hundred square meters or so, hundreds of meters from any regular human activity, and not near any trail. It's a about a 20-minute bushwhack to get there from the nearest road. There's an old homestead clearing about 100 meters from the site.  The only human activity in the area is DNR ecologists performing invasives control at most probably once a year, maybe some occasional hunters, and annual conservation monitoring by Jefferson Land Trust.

Pin location on map is approximate.  The Dabob Bay Natural Area Preserve has expanded beyond the green area indicated on the map baselayer.
severe fern die-off noted. Covers several acres and is patchy in other sites along trail from Fort Ward to Port Blakely.
Western Sword Ferns that appear to be dying off.

In 2016 and 2017 I thought the shabby look to my sword ferns was the result of water stress and thought they would recover the next year.

This spring, after some record breaking snowfall, they are in worse shape.  The article in today's Seattle Times alerted me to the possibility of a more widespread malady.

When I have more time I'll see whether I have some pictures from earlier that I can contrast with their current condition.

column23: Tiger Mountain State Forest, Issaquah, WA, US

column18:
Sword ferns on my property used to be abundant and in good health. Now, several areas of our property show a total decline of ferns while other areas still have ferns but not as many as before. Some fern fronds are turning yellow and most of the plants are flattened. We have done nothing to negatively impact these ferns.
  I have also noticed that there is a much bigger presence of different kinds of mosses on every plant in the yard. My azaleas are covered in hanging moss. This change has occurred in the last couple of years.
  We have an acre of natural NW forest around our house and I have noticed it is not regenerating itself at all. We have lost many trees due to old age, but no new trees are taking their places.
These ferns appeared to be dying, all of them in about a 15 meter radius
Sword ferns uniformly flattened, with little or no new growth. Is this normal for time of year?

Warning: use caution as this is a former mining area.  There are a couple large sinkholes with-in the die-off area and sinkholes have been known to vent dangerous fumes elsewhere in the park. 

This area of sword fern (Polystichum munitum) die-off was first recorded by myself almost one year ago.  The range of this die-off (in which at least 2/3 of specimens have died) is roughly 75'x95’, and I would estimate that the inner 2/3 of that area has 100% death (minus one individual that is nonetheless thriving at dead center).  The die-off seems be spreading somewhat sporadically in the areas beyond the edges of the area.  

On the other side of a small rise to the southwest is a broad gully that seems to have sword fern mortality that is far higher than the surrounding park area.  I don’t know if I would characterize it as a part of the sword fern die-off phenomenon that has been the subject of recent study.  I didn’t not notice this last year, though having kept a close eye on the ground as I passed through there previously. 

I would characterize this area as being first colonized by red alder but mostly taken over big leaf maple.  In the die-off the understory is still very thin but has widespread Galium and a woody plant with a sprawling ivy-like growth form; I don’t recognize the latter, and I suspect that it is an exotic that I have seen aggressively invading the edges of parks in the region.  I believe that I saw it here last year.  

Contrary to the other nearby die-off area noted on the same day, I saw no sign of mountain beaver. 

This area of the park seems to be hit harder with recent drought than other parts, judging by high western hemlock mortality.  Most smaller trees are dead and the larger trees are suffering.  Judging by the relative lack of lichen on the bare twigs, I would guess that they died recently.

This is my first time finding this particular sword fern (Polystichum munitum) die-off area; I do not know if it has previously been noted.  

Just around the bend from Coal Creek Falls, this die-off area (2/3+ dead) is 75'x200'.  It runs parallel along a hillside between the trail and a disused road.  The road is the upper boundary.  The area of 100% sword fern death within the die-off area is about 50'x75', and heavily populated with mountain beaver burrows - some old, some fresh.  I do not recall seeing any sword fern hold-outs within in the area of complete die-off.  I would say that the fairly closed canopy is mostly red alder, about 1/3 western hemlock and douglas fir, and a little bit of big leaf maple.  The only woody understory plants in the area of complete die-off is currant (I assume because the little prickles on the stems inhibit mountain beaver predation).  Judging by the abundance of terrestrial mosses and slower spreading vascular plants like miner's lettuce, I would guess that this site is relatively old; perhaps older than Seward (though Seward's thicker conifer canopy would slow understory growth).  

Contrary to the other die-off area that I observed in the park on the same day, western hemlock mortality is lower in this area and this leads me to think that drought pressure is lower.

On NF-5620 to Dingford Creek Trailhead, this small apparent sword fern die off area occurs under closed canopy on the roadside; hemlocks to east and west and deciduous above to the north and south along the road.  There is clear evidence of die off on the east (uphill) side only, on either side of a gentle ditch which doesn’t appear to be a recent disturbance.

column18: The ground was wet. Very windy.

The ground was wet. There was a fence that looked to be preventing sunlight from hitting the plant directly. There was nothing above the plant, allowing it to get water. The plant looked to be natural but had recently been cut to allow people to walk on the pathway. The plant was green but had spots of brown probably from dehydration.

The ground was wet. The Fern was shoved up against the side of a house. The fern looked to be weak, as it was crumpled over, presumably from the weight of the rain the night before. There were many weeds growing around the edge of the fern. The plant could clearly get water because it was soaked, but the plant could not get sunlight os easy, as it was on the opposite side of the house from the sun.

The ground was wet. It was very windy outside. There was a fence to block the wind from hitting the fern but the fence also prevented the fern from getting much sun. The fern could easily access water, as there was nothing above it and it was soaked from last night's rain. The fern was in the middle of a pathway and looked like it could easily be stepped on.

The sword fern looked to be planted or moved. It was in a pot under a grapevine, making it harder for the plant to get water. The plant was easily able to get sun as there was nothing around it except for the grapevine above. There was a house preventing the sun from reaching the plant but only in the early hours of the morning.
